disease.name: Radiation Enteritis/Colitis
disease.note: Acute or chronic GI injury from ionizing radiation (pelvic/abdominal radiotherapy). Acute: self-limited during RT. Chronic (6mo-5yr post-RT): progressive obliterative endarteritis → ischemia → telangiectasias → bleeding, stricture, fistula. Pelvic RT (prostate, cervix, bladder, rectal cancer) most commonly causes radiation proctitis with hematochezia.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Massive bleed -> URGENT endoscopy/intervention; mild chronic -> APC ablation

subtypes.name: Acute Radiation Proctitis
subtypes.notes: Onset during RT, self-limited, resolves 2-6wk post-RT

subtypes.name: Chronic Radiation Proctitis
subtypes.notes: Onset 6mo-5yr post-RT, telangiectasias, bleeding, may progress to stricture/fistula

subtypes.name: Radiation Enteritis (Small Bowel)
subtypes.notes: More common with abdominal RT, malabsorption, obstruction

firstLine.test: Colonoscopy/flexible sigmoidoscopy (telangiectasias, friable mucosa, pallor, ulceration)

secondLine.test: CT abdomen (bowel wall thickening, stricture, fistula)
Biopsy (obliterative endarteritis, fibrosis, atypical fibroblasts - no malignancy)

investigations.goldStandard: Endoscopy with characteristic telangiectasias and friable mucosa in radiation field

emergency: IV fluids and transfusion if massive bleed
inpatient: APC (argon plasma coagulation) - most effective for bleeding telangiectasias, multiple sessions
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (promotes angiogenesis, improves healing)
outpatient: APC ablation for chronic bleeding telangiectasias
Topical sucralfate or short-chain fatty acid enemas
refractory: Surgical resection for fistula, refractory bleeding, or obstruction
Diverting stoma for severe refractory proctitis
preventive: IMRT (intensity-modulated RT) to reduce bowel dose
Belly board/prone positioning for pelvic RT
Hydrogel spacers between prostate and rectum

disease.pathophysiology: Ionizing radiation → acute: DNA damage to crypt cells → mucosal denudation → acute inflammation → self-limited bleeding. Chronic: progressive obliterative endarteritis (endothelial swelling → subendothelial fibrosis → intimal narrowing) → chronic ischemia → mucosal telangiectasias (fragile, bleed easily) → fibrosis → stricture/fistula formation. Telangiectasias are dilated thin-walled vessels that bleed with minimal trauma from stool passage.

complications.complication: Chronic iron deficiency anemia from telangiectasia bleeding

mustNotMiss: Telangiectasias are fragile - even diagnostic biopsies can cause significant bleeding
Biopsy is still essential to exclude recurrent malignancy masquerading as radiation change
APC is first-line treatment for bleeding telangiectasias but requires multiple sessions
Hyperbaric oxygen is effective but requires 20-40 sessions and insurance pre-authorization
Chronic radiation enteritis is progressive - fibrosis can develop years after RT

diagnosticClues.clue: Prior pelvic RT + painless hematochezia with urgency
Multiple rectal telangiectasias on endoscopy in radiation field
Obliterative endarteritis with atypical fibroblasts on biopsy
Temporal relationship: 6 months to 5 years post-radiation

exclusionClues.clue: Acute illness with fever and positive stool culture (infectious)
